data.title: Old Dominion Freight Line: Valuation More Attractive, But Additional Downside Could Be Near
data.description:
Introduction

Old Dominion Freight Line (NASDAQ:ODFL) for the past year has been plagued by headwinds from higher for longer interest rates. As a result, the LTL truck load carrier has seen rising operating costs and lower freight volumes, both of which have impacted ODFL's financials.

Down double-digits in the past year, their valuation and dividend profile may be particularly attractive for some investors despite my hold rating. However, investors should proceed with caution as the operator could see further downside over the near to medium term.

But long-term, I think ODFL's fundamentals remain solid, and they are a stock worth considering on another 10% - 20% price drop. In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, fundamentals, and why the company could see additional selling pressure in the coming months.

Previous Thesis

It's been a while since I last covered Old Dominion Freight Line, assigning the stock a hold rating due to their rich valuation. At the time, ODFL had a forward P/E of 37.01x, appearing severely overvalued. But their fundamentals, including a strong balance sheet and payout ratio of just 27% made them attractive on a pullback.

As a result, I suggested investors wait for a potential price correction or market crash before adding. I also assumed ODFL was at high risk to see downside if President Trump implemented tariffs as promised, as these would result in higher costs, likely negatively impacting their financials.

Since, ODFL has pulled back, down over 16% at the time of writing compared to the S&P (SP500), up almost 2.5% over the same period.

Latest Quarter Shows Lingering Headwinds

Old Dominion Freight Line reported their Q1 2025 earnings back in April, and it was apparent the trucking company has been impacted by a weakening economy. Although, EPS of $1.19 managed to beat analysts' estimates, while revenue was in-line at $1.37 billion.

Even though they displayed resilience by rallying after exceeding expectations, their top and bottom lines declined year-over-year. Revenue declined slightly from $1.4 billion, while their bottom line declined double-digits from $1.34.

For comparison, peer J.B. Hunt Transport (JBHT) also experienced a decline, albeit lesser, in their top and bottom lines. Their revenue declined year-over-year from $2.94 billion to $2.92 billion, while earnings per share fell from $1.22 to $1.17. Their operating income fell 8% from $194.4 million to $178.7 million.

Because of tariffs, the market remains on high alert, which has resulted in less than stellar returns in the first 6 months of the year. Many are also wondering how tariffs will impact the Fed's decision regarding lower interest rates.

Below, you can see how Old Dominion's revenue was on an uptrend until 2022, when the Fed decided to hike interest rates. Ever since, revenue has been on a slight decline. But long-term, I expect ODFL to see solid growth once interest rates are much lower, likely over the next 1 to 2 years.

If future tariffs are implemented, or these have a delayed impact, we could see a potential resurgence in inflation sometime in the back half of the year. If so, this will have a negative impact on many companies, including ODFL.

Old Dominion's management attributed their financial decline to a 5% decrease in LTL shipments and 6.3% decrease in LTL tons. But this was partially offset by a 2.2% increase in LTL revenue.

On a positive note, their operating ratio increased 200 basis points year-over-year from 73.4% to 75.4%, a result of their revenue's deleveraging effect on operating expenses.

Going forward, I expect similar performances for ODFL as they remain pressured by higher costs and financially constrained consumers. As a result, freight volumes and demand will likely remain impacted for the near to medium term.

Reduced CAPEX

As a result of a tough macro environment, Old Dominion Freight Line was forced to revise their capital expenditure guidance this year by $125 million to $450 million. The last two years, the company spent a significantly more on investments, amounting to $771 million in 2024 and $757 million in 2023.

Aside from the declining revenue, this is also a tell-tale sign of a tough economic backdrop. Last year, ODFL opened four new service centers and expected to open several more facilities. But until we see a clearer economic backdrop, these will likely be put on hold.

Dividend Profile

Through their tribulations, ODFL's fundamentals have remained solid. Cash from operations, although declining year-over-year, was still solid at $336.5 million. The prior year's quarter saw this at $415.4 million. But as a result of reduced capital expenditures, free cash flow actually came in higher than last year at $248.4 million.

Paying $59.5 million in dividends, this gave ODFL plenty of room for future dividend increases and ensured their dividend will likely remain safe for the foreseeable future.

CAPEX in Q1'24 was $234.7 million, while free cash flow was $180.7 million. For the full-year, free cash flow was $929 million and with CAPEX now reduced, this gives management more retained capital. And this could be used for additional share repurchases for earnings growth, or to pay down long-term debt.

As you can see, Old Dominion's dividend has been well-covered for the past 8 years, giving them plenty of room for future dividend growth, share repurchases, and acquisitions.

Balance Sheet

Old Dominion's balance sheet was solid, with $39.9 billion in long-term debt. However, this may be worrisome for some investors as this currently exceeds their market cap of $33.7 billion.

But long-term debt has improved tremendously over the past 3 years where this stood at roughly $100 billion. Moreover, their ample liquidity after dividend payments gives them additional access to capital to pay down debt over time.

Fairly Valued

After what, I thought, was a justified price correction, ODFL's forward P/E may still be considered high at 30.59x currently. This is down from 37.01x, where they traded at the end of last year. According to FAST Graphs, this suggests ODFL is currently fairly valued but could see potential downside according to their end of year price target of $153.26, which I agree.

As previously mentioned, I think Old Dominion Freight Line will see strong price appreciation over the long term, but due to increased uncertainty that could result in further downside, I now think investors should consider adding below $150.

Risks

Goldman Sachs recently cut its recession probability percentage from 35% to 30%. And while we could avoid one, I think the weakening economy and tariffs could push us into a downturn in the next 12 months.

Moreover, immigration deportations and negative GDP could be a sign of the times. While I'm not predicting a recession, I think chances are higher than 30%, and more around 50% to 60% in the next year.

On the flip side, interest rates will likely remain elevated, at least for the next 6 to 12 months. And while there are two rate cuts expected by the end of this year, I wouldn't be surprised to see one rate cut instead.

Bottom Line

Old Dominion Freight Line, despite their decline year-over-year, has shown resilience as a result of their strong fundamentals. Moreover, their dividend remains as safe due to their reduced spending expected this year.

With a free cash flow payout ratio of less than 30%, this gives the LTL operator more than enough capital to navigate current headwinds and make investments for future growth. As a result of their resilience, the company still remains expensive in my opinion, with a forward P/E above 30x.

While their quality suggests a higher multiple, economic uncertainty could result in further downside, where I think the stock will be much more attractive. For investors looking to add, I suggest doing so on any share price weakness for a margin of safety.

data.title: 3 Big Dividend Income Machines Printing Cash For Retirement
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is one of the best dividend income machines for someone looking to live off cash flow from their investments to fund their retirement. This is because, in addition to offering around a 4% dividend yield, it has a very impressive dividend growth track record of an 11% CAGR over the past decade. This means that not only is its current yield pretty substantial compared to broader market index funds, but it also has a history of growing at a pace that significantly outpaces inflation.

Additionally, its long-term total return performance is pretty solid, indicating that it is more than just a passive income generator but also a wealth compounder over time.

This is in large part thanks to its very low 0.06% expense ratio, as well as its portfolio of over 100 blue-chip dividend growth stocks.

That being said, SCHD does have some shortcomings, including a lack of much in the way of exposure to infrastructure as well as mega-cap tech stocks, meaning that it may be under-diversified. Additionally, its yield of ~4%, while higher than what you can get from many broadly diversified index funds or even other dividend growth funds like the Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), is still not terribly high for someone who is in retirement mode and is focused primarily on generating passive income from dividends rather than maximizing capital appreciation.

With these two shortcomings in mind, in this article, I am going to share three big dividend income machines that can significantly enhance a portfolio centered on SCHD from both a yield and diversification standpoint.

Big Dividend Income Machine #1

The first one I am going to discuss is one that provides significant exposure to infrastructure, and is called the Cohen and Steers Infrastructure CEF (UTF). It has a broadly diversified portfolio spread across multiple sectors, including utilities and other infrastructure businesses, including in the industrial and energy spaces. It also has a small amount of fixed income and real estate exposure.

Overall, there is a lot to like as it only trades at a slight premium to its net asset value, meaning that investors do not have to overpay much for the fund. Additionally, it pays out a very attractive 7.2% yield with monthly distributions that have been remarkably consistent over the long-term history of the fund. Moreover, its long-term performance has been very strong, with it outperforming its broader index over the past decade by an annualized rate of about 260 basis points, and it has also outperformed the utilities sector (XLU) over that period:

This demonstrates significant alpha on the part of the management team in allocating capital across the infrastructure space. With this fund, investors can significantly enhance their yield and diversification based on a portfolio centered on SCHD.

Big Dividend Income Machine #2

The next fund I am going to discuss is the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP). This fund works alongside UTF in providing diversified exposure to infrastructure while also offering an even higher dividend yield of 7.9%. The reason I like AMLP, in addition to its high yield, is that it has strong dividend growth momentum going on right now, as over the past three years it has a 10.06% dividend CAGR, and over the past year, its dividend growth rate has been 8.78%.

Its ability to generate not only a high current yield but inflation-beating dividend growth is likely to continue for the foreseeable future as its top holdings include the likes of Energy Transfer (ET), MPLX (MPLX), Plains All-American Pipeline (PAA), and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), all of which are likely to grow their dividends at a pace that meets or beats inflation for the foreseeable future. It also has some smaller holdings like Genesis Energy (GEL) and Hess Midstream (HESM) that have also delivered guidance for strong distribution growth in the coming years.

While it is not as well-diversified as UTF is, its higher yield and higher dividend growth potential make AMLP a compelling addition to a retirement income-focused portfolio that wants to increase its exposure to real assets and increase its overall yield while not sacrificing its ability to deliver inflation-beating dividend growth.

Big Dividend Income Machine #3

The third fund I will discuss in this article is the J.P. Morgan Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ). This fund focuses on holding a Nasdaq-100-like underlying portfolio and then implements a notional covered call strategy overlaid on top of it. This is done to generate very attractive monthly distributions, and with an 11.55% trailing 12-month yield, this fund's capacity to deliver very attractive dividends should be evident. With an expense ratio of just 0.35% and the ability to combine yield enhancement with significant diversification enhancement, given that 50% of its holdings are in the mega cap tech space—which the other three funds discussed in this article lack—JEPQ's value to a portfolio like this should be quite evident.

That being said, it is also worth noting that its covered call strategy means that it does not participate much in strong rallies, while still participating fully in the downside of its underlying holdings. This means that it is at risk of long-term NAV erosion. Therefore, I do not think it is prudent to have too much allocated to this fund, and would keep it as a relatively small holding in a portfolio. At the same time, though, the diversification and yield it provides are valuable given that exposure to mega-cap tech is often otherwise lacking in an income-focused portfolio, so JPQ could be a worthwhile holding for that reason alone.

Investor Takeaway

As this article points out, SCHD can be a very powerful foundational holding in a dividend retirement portfolio. That said, its yield could certainly be higher, and it lacks diversification to real assets like infrastructure and mega-cap tech stocks. To solve these issues, adding funds like UTF, AMLP, and JEPQ can enhance a retirement income portfolio focused on SCHD as its core holding. While we do not invest in many funds and favor picking individual stocks, this sort of balance between yield, dividend growth, and diversification is core to our strategy for formulating our retirement portfolio at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Negotiating Window For Iran
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Two weeks

President Donald Trump has once again set a two-week timeframe to decide on a complex issue - this time on whether the U.S. should launch an attack on Iran. The deadline is aimed at pressuring Tehran to come to the negotiating table for a nuclear deal, as the escalating Israel-Iran conflict enters its second week.

Diplomacy first: "Based on the fact that there's a substantial chance of negotiations that may or may not take place with Iran in the near future, I will make my decision whether or not to go within the next two weeks," Trump said in a message delivered by White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt. The deadline comes as Israel and Iran continue to exchange attacks by air, targeting military and nuclear sites, as well as civilian infrastructure.

British, French, German and EU foreign ministers will meet with their Iranian counterpart in Geneva on Friday to discuss the situation. "The president supports diplomatic efforts from our allies that could bring Iran closer to taking his deal," a White House official said. While diplomatic efforts are ongoing, Trump weighing a potential strike on Iran has split his support base in the U.S., as many allies want to avoid a repeat of the 2003 invasion of Iraq.

Eye on oil: Iran is reportedly racing to get its oil out into the world, as satellite images show storage tanks at the Kharg Island export terminal are brimming with crude. At the same time, Iran has been driving up its crude exports. This likely means that Iran is directing more crude into the facility to ensure that it can send as much oil as possible to the global market. Meanwhile, energy and shipping industries are closely watching developments at the Strait of Hormuz. The waterway "is the artery through which the world's energy flows, and if that artery is blocked, it'll have a huge impact on global trade," Shell (SHEL) CEO Wael Sawan warned.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

2 BDCs To Buy Before Rates Drop

SCHD: Please Know What You Own

If I Could Only Retire With 3 High Yields Right Now

Nvidia: All-Time High Is In Sight, Upgrade To Strong Buy

Get In Before It's Obvious: The Biggest Bull Market Of The Decade Could Already Be Underway

What else is happening...

SoftBank's Son pitches $1T AI hub to TSMC (TSM), Trump team.

Musk's X eyes in-app stock trading, more consolidation ahead?

Mars mission: SpaceX's (SPACE) Starship explodes during test.

Amazon (AMZN) directs staff to relocate to be near managers.

Trump extends deadline for TikTok (BDNCE) ban for third time.

Bidding war? Home Depot (HD) makes offer for GMS (GMS)

Biggest sports deal: LA Lakers set to be sold at $10B valuation.

Gilead (GILD) wins FDA nod for twice-yearly HIV PrEP shot.

China's rare earth magnet exports slump to five-year low in May.

Swiss central bank's rate hits zero, Bank of England holds steady.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -0.2%. Hong Kong +1.3%. China -0.1%. India +1.3%.
In Europe, at midday, London +0.4%. Paris +0.5%. Frankfurt +0.9%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -0.3%. S&P -0.3%. Nasdaq -0.3%. Crude +0.5% to $73.85. Gold -1% to $3,372.90. Bitcoin +1% to $106,058.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +3 bps to 4.42%.

On The Calendar

Companies reporting today include Accenture (ACN) and Kroger (KR).

See the full earnings calendar on Seeking Alpha, as well as today's economic calendar.

data.title: Mowi: Came For Consumer Products, Stayed For The Secular Moat
data.description:
I am an unsophisticated investor. When I find an interesting product on the store shelves, I go on to research the company producing it and conduct the analysis. That is how I stumbled across Mowi ASA (OTCPK:MHGVY) (OTCPK:MNHVF). Coming back from Japan, I was desperately missing Onigiri (rice ball) snacks in Europe, which were all over Japanese 7-Eleven stores (which I also cover). One day, Mowi Onigiri appeared on European supermarket shelves, albeit with Salmon, a type of fish actually not even common in Japan. Nevertheless, read on to discover what kept me hooked to Mowi from an investor's perspective, far beyond these Consumer Products.

Understanding Mowi

Mowi is the world's largest producer of Atlantic Salmon, and has the complete value chain vertically integrated within the company, including Feed, Breeding, Farming, Harvesting, Processing and Selling including Marketing. However, note that the Feed division is currently under strategic review. Mowi's main market is Europe (66%), followed by America (22%) and Asia (10%).

With its full vertical integration naturally come lots of internal revenues, which also depend on the valuation of goods traded within the company. This, from my perspective, makes it difficult to meaningfully assess segment margins, which I consider a minor issue here. See below, for example, how in the Feed and Farming segments, most revenue is internal, while the Consumer Products segment generates the company's main external revenue. The Markets segment shows an approximate 50:50 split. This also explains why out of roughly 500k tons of produced volume, only 232k tons are reported in the Consumer Products segment. The other half must therefore be sold externally through the Markets segment.

Securing More Of The Rare Production Capacity

Part one of Mowi's value driver equation is ever-increasing production and capacity growth, with a 5% CAGR over the past 6 years to 513k tons on a TTM basis. Norway has held an average of 60% of overall production but contributes an even higher EBIT-share of up to more than 80% in some quarters. The forecasted production CAGR from 2024 to 2029 continues to be 5% to 650k tons. This includes the still ongoing Nova Sea acquisition, which is expected to be closed by H2-2025.

Scarcity-Driven Price

While production volumes depict a long-term CAGR of 5%, revenue grew an average of 7% over the past 6 years due to favorable salmon price developments. But the trend reversed for the time being, with 2% revenue growth against 6% production growth in 2024, as salmon prices decreased. See below the implied realized price in Euro per Kilogram sold by Mowi over time. Over the longer term, however, salmon prices tend to move upwards.

The short-term pressure on prices was due to global supply catching up to record levels recently. But that does not change the management's expectations of prices regaining traction in the long term, starting in 2026 at the latest. This is because Mowi expects the industry to be able to grow supply by only 2-3% annually, lagging global growth in demand. Mowi mentions a historically strong correlation between low supply growth and increasing salmon prices.

The company guides for revenue of €8.5 billion in 2029, implying a 9% top-line CAGR starting from 2024's €5.6 billion. Returning to the anticipated 5% volume CAGR earlier, the remaining 4% annually must come from price increases through branding, product development and the aforementioned scarcity due to limited supply growth. "(…) the industry has reached a production level where biological boundaries are being pushed (…)", Mowi writes in its Salmon Industry Handbook. After all, for optimal development, salmon requires water temperatures of 8 to 14 degrees Celsius and a certain level of current to flow through the farm. These conditions are not found at any given coast and provide a moat for Mowi as the market leader.

Salmon prices also remained more robust than other proteins during the 2023 decline, further cementing its historical position as a rather expensive form of protein. Demand is supported by salmon generally being viewed as healthy, as it is, for example, rich in Omega-3, protein, vitamins, minerals or antioxidants. Mowi also claims that salmon has the best climate footprint among all animal proteins.

Production Costs

Mowi's main P&L line item is Cost of Materials with 50% of revenue, mainly consisting of Feed; followed by personnel expenses and other operating expenses, all at comparably low levels, well below 20%. For the first quarter, Mowi reported blended farming costs of €5.89 per kilogram, down from €6.05 a year ago. This represents a welcome downward trend after inflationary spikes starting in the 2021 post-Covid period. Higher volumes should lead to continued production cost decreases in Q2 and H2, according to Mowi.

The company also reported cost improvement potential of €300-400 million. Related to 2029's expected revenue of €8.5 billion, this would translate to a margin improvement of 4 percentage points. See below, that major margin improvements are already factored into analysts' forecasts.

Consumer Products

Mowi's Consumer Products likely capture the industry's Zeitgeist, and they were the starting point of my analysis of Mowi. However, they are not what keep me particularly interested in the company, as operating margins in the segment are in razor-thin, low single digit margin territory, albeit improving to 3.7% in Q1-2025, up from 2.7% in Q1-2024. Volume was up 3% while revenue was essentially flat due to an adverse product mix.

Risk Summary

Net Debt has been raised as a reason for concern among some analysts. However, the company's Net Debt to EBITDA ratio of 2.9 on a TTM basis according to Seeking Alpha appears manageable, as does an interest coverage ratio of 4.8 (EBIT / Net Interest Expenses) for the moment. The company's cash balance is typically low due to high net working capital requirements and lengthy farming cycles, paired with growth efforts.

Externally, salmon health and conditions such as seawater temperatures pose constant risks to the business.

Valuation Is Not An Obstacle

Mowi's long-term earnings and revenue CAGRs resemble those of its Norwegian peers, as largely do the expected ones. Refer to the table below for details. On a 2027 forward earnings basis, Mowi trades at a 10x multiple today, within the peers' forward P/E range of 7 to 11. This indicates a fair valuation, making it an adequate entry point.

Against the background of the expected scarcity-driven price dynamics in favor of salmon producers, I am considering initiating a position in this global market leader. The reason for the still cautious Hold rating is that its commodity-like business model is not what I have high conviction about in a classic buy-and-hold portfolio. Also, regulatory and climate related risks including optimal farming conditions keep me from rating an unrestricted Buy. Still, my inclination leans positive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Home Depot Reportedly Makes Move On GMS
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Home Depot (HD) makes its own bid for GMS - report. (00:25) Trump to decide on Iran in two weeks; Tehran reportedly rushing to export oil. (01:04) SoftBank's Masayoshi Son is said to propose $1 trillion U.S. AI hub to TSMC (TSM) and Trump team. (01:45)

This is an abridged transcript.

Home Depot (NYSE:HD) has reportedly made an offer for GMS (NYSE:GMS).

This follows QXO's (QXO) bid on Wednesday for GMS for $95.20 per share, for a transaction value of ~$5B.

The Wall Street Journal reported that Home Depot's offer is unclear at this point.

GMS distributes wallboard, ceilings, steel framing, and construction products, as well as commercial and residential building materials. It operates through tool sales, rental, and service centers, as well as a network of distribution centers.

GMS is up 19% in premarket action.

President Donald Trump will decide within the next two weeks if the U.S. should launch an attack on Iran, pressuring Tehran to the negotiating table for a nuclear deal.

The president said in a message delivered by the press secretary, "Based on the fact that there's a substantial chance of negotiations that may or may not take place with Iran in the near future, I will make my decision whether or not to go within the next two weeks."

The deadline comes as the escalating Israel-Iran conflict enters its second week. Israel struck 100 targets in Iran, including nuclear sites, on Thursday. Iran continues to launch missiles and drones at Israel.

Masayoshi Son is looking to team up with Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (NYSE:TSM) to realize what could be his biggest bet yet — a trillion-dollar industrial complex in Arizona to build robots and artificial intelligence.

Bloomberg reported that the founder of SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) (OTCPK:SFTBF) envisions a version of the vast manufacturing hub of China’s Shenzhen that would bring back high-tech manufacturing to the US.

The park may comprise production lines for AI-powered industrial robots.

The company is making a bold push into artificial intelligence, pledging up to $40 billion in investments toward OpenAI—starting with an initial $8.5 billion through its Vision Fund 2.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Stellantis and Renault urge EU to ease regulations amid rising competition from China's small cars

Meta plans to hire Safe Superintelligence CEO Daniel Gross and Nat Friedman to push AI - report

BBC threatens legal action versus Perplexity over alleged use of content to train AI models - report

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the green. Crude oil is up 0.9% at $74/barrel. Bitcoin is up 1.2% at $105,000. Gold is down 0.3% at $3,358.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 0.9%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Smith & Wesson (NASDAQ:SWBI) -12% - Shares fell after the firearm maker reported weaker-than-expected FQ4 results on Wednesday, citing inflation, high-interest rates, and trade policy uncertainty.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Philadelphia Fed Manufacturing Index

  • 10:00 am Leading Indicators

data.title: Chart Of The Day: We've Lost Our Bears... And That's A Problem
data.description:
By Mike Larson

We’ve lost our bears... and that might be a problem.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows the percentage of respondents to the weekly American Association of Individual Investors (AAII) survey who said they were bearish on stocks. This bearish sentiment indicator surged to almost 62% back in early April. But it just plummeted to less than 34%.

AAII Bearish % Sentiment Indicator

(Data by YCharts)

You can see a similar pattern in other investor sentiment indicators, including the CNN Fear & Greed Index. And of course, the CBOE Volatility Index (VIX) has come way down from its peak of 52.3 on April 8. It closed at 20.1 Wednesday - above the low under 17 from early June, thanks to the Middle East crisis... but far from a "panicky" reading.

What’s the problem? Isn’t optimism good for us? Maybe in real life. But not necessarily in markets.

You might remember that back in late April, I highlighted how bearish sentiment readings remained very high despite a big rally back from the “Liberation Day Massacre.” Investors didn’t really “believe” the rally.

From a contrarian standpoint, that meant it would likely continue. Late-comers and FOMO buyers were still waiting in the wings, providing fuel for more gains if they capitulated and bought. The latest data and market activity suggests that’s exactly what has happened.

Now, we don’t have that cushion of FOMO money. The bears are MIA. So, even if headlines out of the Middle East get less ominous in the coming days, markets may struggle to get much done.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wells Fargo Is A Compliance-Constrained Compounder Now Set Free
data.description:
Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) is still discounted like a penalty-box bank. But the fundamentals are already telling a different story. Credit is stable. The capital stack is pristine. The balance sheet is overbuilt, not under fire. And the bank is buying back stock like it actually believes in itself. Which, frankly, it should.

This isn't 2016. And it's definitely not 2020. The fines have been paid. The legacy issues are being cleared. Twelve consent orders are gone, six of them in 2025 alone. Most investors spent the past seven years treating the Fed's asset cap like a guillotine. Now it's officially gone, and that changes the calculus. Wells is no longer constrained. It's a structurally advantaged compounder with the green light to scale.

Company Overview

Wells Fargo doesn't need much of an introduction. It's one of the most entrenched names in American banking, with a branch network that stretches across the country and a customer base that includes tens of millions. The firm's business is broad but familiar, traditional consumer lending, small business banking, commercial credit, investment banking, and wealth management. Those are broken into four formal segments, but the real story is simpler: Wells runs one of the largest deposit-taking franchises in the country and pairs that scale with a mix of fee income and credit products that touch every part of the U.S. economy. Consumer and commercial banking drive the core earnings engine, while WIM and CIB round out the fee income profile.

The 2016 fake account scandal cratered its reputation, drew the Fed asset cap, and led to nearly a decade of cleanup. But since Charles Scharf took over in 2019, Wells has rebuilt its compliance architecture, consolidated risk controls, and largely moved past the mess. The market has noticed. The stock rose ~44% in 2024 alone. But it still trades at a multiple that implies it's a wounded peer, not a structurally improving franchise. That disconnect is the opportunity.

Financial Deep Dive

Start with the numbers. Q1 2025 net income was $4.9 billion, up 6% year-over-year. EPS came in at $1.39, up 16%, thanks to aggressive buybacks and tighter cost control. Return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) hit 13.6%. That's not JPMorgan territory, but it's not a mid-tier bank either.

Net interest income (NII) was $11.5 billion, down 6% YoY, driven by a softer NIM of 2.67% as deposit costs caught up to asset yields. But the curve is already shifting. Fee income held up, even with some softness in equity-related gains. Stronger contributions from asset management and investment banking helped smooth things out. On the credit side, conditions remained stable. Charge-offs came down from the prior quarter, landing at 0.45%, and there's no real sign of pressure building in the loan book. The reserve ratio is still over 1.5% of loans. Nothing in the credit book is blinking red.

Expenses fell 3% YoY. That matters. Scharf has promised a more efficient Wells, and it's starting to show up in the numbers. The efficiency ratio was 69%, not amazing, but steadily improving.

Segment performance was resilient across the board. Consumer banking held steady on deposit volumes and fee income. Cards were up. Home lending was flat. Auto is being wound down on purpose. Commercial banking posted $2.93B in revenue, down modestly due to margin compression. But treasury fees rose, and provisions for commercial real estate (CRE) actually declined quarter over quarter. In CIB, revenue rose 2% to $5.06B, with trading and investment banking both contributing. In WIM, revenue rose 4% to $3.87B, with client assets hitting $2.23 trillion. Deposits in the wealth channel rose 22% YoY. Read that again.

Valuation

At ~$75 per share, WFC trades at 12.5x 2025 earnings and 1.45x book value. That's not expensive. Tangible book is in the low $40s, so you're looking at ~1.7x TBV for a franchise delivering 13%+ ROTCE. That's a discount to historical norms. Before 2016, Wells routinely traded at 2.0x+ book because it deserved to. It doesn't yet, but it could again.

You don't need a hero's multiple. If earnings grow 10% annually for the next two years, a pace below Street expectations, and the P/E holds at 12.5x, you're still looking at a mid-teens total return when you include the dividend.

You can also run a segment valuation. WIM could command a 14x multiple on earnings, in line with peers like Morgan Stanley. Core banking can easily support 12x, especially with ROTCE north of 13%. Sum it out, and the stock isn't overvalued. It's discounted.

Capital Return

This is where the story accelerates. In Q1 alone, Wells returned $4.8 billion to shareholders, $1.3B in dividends and $3.5B in buybacks. That's a 100% payout. Over the past year, Wells has repurchased 10% of its share count. ROTCE is rising in part because the equity base is shrinking fast. Book value per share is up 7% year-over-year. The dividend yield is 2.2%, modest, but growing. Buybacks are doing the heavy lifting.

The total shareholder yield here is north of 8%. And it's sustainable. CET1 stands at 11.1%, well above regulatory minimums. The LCR is 125%. Liquidity is abundant. Even in a stressed scenario, Wells can keep sending cash back to shareholders. Few other banks have that kind of flexibility.

Catalysts

The biggest lever just triggered. As of June 3, 2025, the Fed officially lifted Wells Fargo's asset cap after more than seven years of regulatory constraint. It's hard to overstate the impact. For years, Wells has been running below capacity, forced to shrink certain loan books, limit deposit inflows, and operate under a ceiling no peer bank faced. That's now gone. The bank can finally grow again. It can take in new deposits, expand lending, and rebuild its asset base organically. It also removes a reputational drag that's lingered since 2018. This isn't theoretical anymore. The cap is gone, and the next few quarters will reflect what Wells looks like when it's no longer handcuffed.

Second, efficiency gains. Every 100 bps of improvement in the efficiency ratio drops ~$200 million to the bottom line. If they can move from 69% to 63%, that's over a billion in pre-tax earnings. And that's before you account for the operating leverage from better revenue growth.

Third, the rate backdrop is finally normalizing. Deposit betas are peaking. If the Fed cuts in late 2025 or 2026, Wells' funding costs fall faster than loan yields. That could stabilize or even lift NIM from here. NII might have bottomed.

Risks

CRE remains a wildcard. Wells has ~$29 billion in office loans, and non-accruals are elevated. But the exposure is manageable. The reserve is there. Charge-offs actually declined last quarter.

Regulatory risk is always in play. While the asset cap is gone, any major misstep could reignite supervisory pressure or trigger new restrictions. The margin for error remains tight.

Rate volatility could sting. If the Fed slashes rates too fast, NIM could compress before funding costs reset. But with so many levers, Wells has more tools than most.

The larger shadow risk is reputational. Wells Fargo doesn't just have a regulatory history, it has a track record of high-profile, prolonged misconduct. From the 2016 fake account scandal to mortgage overcharging, improper auto insurance sales, and whistleblower retaliation, Wells has amassed a litany of compliance failures that went far beyond a single bad quarter or executive misstep. It wasn't one issue, it was systemic. The result is that investors, regulators, and the public carry a heightened sensitivity to any headline involving the brand. That creates asymmetric downside. If JPMorgan stumbles, it's a story. If Wells stumbles, it's a referendum. That perception risk doesn't show up in a credit reserve or capital ratio, but it absolutely shows up in market sentiment. Any minor operational lapse, no matter how well-contained, risks being interpreted as evidence of relapse. It could reinstate regulatory caps, spook large clients, and choke momentum before it has a chance to compound. The irony is that the company may now be one of the most tightly supervised and risk-controlled franchises in the country, but legacy baggage means the margin for public error is still razor-thin. Wells doesn't get to mess up quietly.

Conclusion

Wells Fargo is what it says it is. A $1.4 trillion deposit machine with a rising wealth business, a stable commercial franchise, a credible investment bank, and a leadership team that's finally earned some benefit of the doubt. The balance sheet is overbuilt. Capital is excessive. Credit is clean. And the stock is still priced like the next scandal is around the corner.

It's not. The worst is behind it.

The cap is gone. That re-rating process is already underway. In the meantime, you're still getting an 8%+ shareholder yield while ROTCE trends higher. This isn't a turnaround story anymore. It's a fully operational compounder that just got permission to accelerate.

Wells Fargo is a Buy.

data.title: Black Hills: Stable Profitability And Cheap Stock, But There's Quantitative Risk
data.description:
Black Hills Corporation (NYSE:BKH) is a utility company serving 1.35 million customers in eight states. The company is projected to deliver stable financial performance through the first quarter of 2025 with EPS at $1.87 while revenue growth of 10% to $805.2 million. In this article, I'll provide a detailed analysis of BKH, which produced 55 consecutive years of dividend growth and stable income, and explain why I am a 'Hold'.

Financials

BKH's revenue was in a certain seasonality since 2019. The company announces its highest revenue in the first quarter of the year every year. While the trend was on the positive side until 2023, revenue started to do lower high after 2024 and BKH entered a slightly complicated position.

But the situation is not like that in net profit. The rise in revenue stopped, but the rise in net income continues. In the first quarter of each year, net profit grows by an average of 7.92% YoY. The situation did not change in the first quarter of 2025 and net profit grew by 5% YoY. So even though the growth from YoY was somewhat disrupted on the income side, the positive trend in net profit continues.

This sharp seasonality makes it easier for me to predict future quarters. I calculate an average of 42% QoQ decline in revenue in the second quarter of each year. When I take into account that this much decline will occur in the next quarter, I calculate revenue as $466.6 million for the second quarter.

But another thing to consider here is the YoY growth. Announcing revenue as $466.6 million in the second quarter means growth of close to 16% YoY, which is not a conservative figure. Because since 2019, the average YoY growth in the second quarter was 7.69%. So when an average annual growth rate is announced, I calculate that we should expect an average of $433.6 in revenue. So my average revenue expectation for Q2 is $450.1.

The average net margin in Q2s since 2019 was 5.98%. Again, I see the same seasonality in net margin. With this level of net margin, I predict net profit in the next quarter to be $26.9 million. With 73 million shares outstanding, this corresponds to approximately $0.37 EPS. Considering that the analyst consensus on BKH's Seeking Alpha page is $412.7 for revenue and $0.39 for EPS, I think BKH will announce above expected revenue but below expected EPS in the next quarter. While the revenue exceeded expectations even in the most conservative case, the fact that EPS was around expectations shows me that there is a potential for a surprise in EPS and an announcement that exceeds expectations. But since the calculations yielded this result, I have to say this.

Dividend Analysis

BKH is a company that distributes dividends. When I do dividend analysis since 2010, I don't see a bad picture. BKH still continues to grow dividend it's paying for 53 years without failing. The rate of this growth 4.3% on average. When normalized for inflation, I can say that there is mostly real growth. The growth rate was below inflation only in 2012 and the post-COVID period of 2022-2023. Real growth was seen outside these periods. So BKH seems to be a very suitable investment for dividend investors.

I calculate the CAGR of dividend growth as 3.35% (5Y). So, with this calculation, dividend per share forecasted to be $3.18 in 2030. I see that DPS growth was slowing down in a certain trend, especially since 2017. If this trend continues, I calculate that DPS growth will fall to 1.37% in 2030 and therefore DPS will be at $3.02. I can say that I expect an average EPS of $3.10 in 2030.

When I look at dividend yield, I see a sharp decline from 8% in 2010 to 4.3% in 2014. Since this date, the dividend yield has remained at an average of 4.18%. At the same time, I see that the trend that continued until 2019 reversed in 2019 and the dividend yield is now slowly increasing from 3.2% to 5%.

When linear regression analysis is performed, I can calculate that the stock price will be $50.94 in 2030 with a dividend yield of 6.09% and an EPS of $3.10. But if the dividend yield does not continue in this trend and approaches the average again, I calculate that the stock price will be around $74.15 in 2030 with a dividend yield of 4.18%. So the dividend analysis shows me that the stock price will be in the range of $51-74 in 2030.

Forward P/E

I said that the BKH's profitability is quite stable. While net profit remained stable, especially since 2020, the downtrend in the share price seriously cheapened the stock and continues to do so. When I look at the Forward P/E since 2016, I clearly see the downtrend that started, especially in 2019.

Currently, BKH's forward P/E is at one of the lowest positions it was since 2016. I calculate the historical average of its forward P/E as 16.88x. Currently, firm is far below this average at around 13x.

In addition to that, when the forward P/E is compared with the SP500 index, I calculate that there is an average difference of 0.1x between them until 2019. So BKH was almost at the SP500 average. But there was a big divergence after 2019 and the difference is now over 8x. In other words, BKH is currently much cheaper than the SP500 average. So I can say that BKH is a stock that is getting cheaper.

Quantitative Data

When I analyze using the Heston Model, I determine that BKH's expected return in the next 1 year is 4%, while there is a 36.8% downside risk and 38.9% upside potential. Even though median volatility tends to decrease over time, long-term volatility is at 22%. In terms of reference value, I have to say that SP500's long-term volatility is at 17.4% and its expected return is 5.8%.

When I analyze with the Rough Volatility model, I see that the volatility trend continues to increase significantly. So I determine that it is possible for BKH to enter market turbulence by increasing market stress, which increases the risk.

In order to verify this data, I want to analyze BKH's Risk-Adjusted Performance Measures. First of all, BKH has a Sharpe ratio of 0.60, which is above the SP500 average of 0.51. Even though the Sharpe of SP500 was above 2 before March and BKH was still at these levels, BKH currently seems to have the potential to show better risk-adjusted performance than SP500. Its Sortino ratio is almost the same as SP500 at 0.94.

Charting

The same cannot be said for BKH, which emerged strongly from the instability following the 2008 crisis and remained in a strong bull market until 2021. According to the Hodrick–Prescott filter, which I use to evaluate the trend, BKH ended its bull run that going on since 2010 in the summer of 2023, falling to $46. Even though it rose above the filter again at the end of 2024, unfortunately it was affected by the general market trend and could not stay above $61 and faced selling pressure again.

When I apply the price action method, I determine the important support and resistance points as $48 and $61 respectively. Especially in the monthly time frame, the MACD indicator shows that the negative momentum continues. So I think the decline to the support level will continue. But this will not be a direct decline.

Especially when I go down to the weekly time frame, I also see that $55 is a really valuable support. The MACD indicator shows that the negative momentum continues in the weekly time frame. If BKH encounters high volume buying pressure at $55, it can rise to $61 again. But if it is not possible to hold at this level, the $48 support will be waiting for BKH.

In the long term, I think that even if the company continues to be profitable in a stable manner and in addition to this, since the valuations indicate that the stock is cheap, it will hold here and rise to $61 again even if it falls to $48 in the worst-case scenario. So the scenario I have in mind is a side channel formation.

Risks

BKH is already facing regulatory expenditures, therefore complying with stricter standards is anticipated to increase operating expenses. Pending litigation in Nebraska, as well as modifications to Wyoming's wildfire liability regulations, are expected to have an influence on operating flexibility.

Conclusion

As a result, it is found that BKH, a financially stable stock, is extremely inexpensive both historically and in comparison to the index. Even though BKH is cheap, it is also highly volatile and was in a downtrend since 2020. I think BKH, which I think seems like a very suitable stock for dividend investors, will continue this downtrend in the near future, although I think it will be in the side channel in the long term. So my position for BKH is "hold".

data.title: BOTZ: AI Applications Grow At A Rapid Pace
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (NASDAQ:BOTZ). Morgan Stanley recently updated its "Humanoid 100" stock list, adding two more sectors impacted by AI.

We will see that this addition is not just about two isolated cases, but rather an exponential growth phenomenon that should reach many other sectors. Additionally, investors can expose themselves to this through an asset with an attractive valuation, such as the BOTZ ETF.

Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF

BOTZ is a thematic ETF that tracks the Indxx Global Robotics & Artificial Intelligence Thematic Index. BOTZ provides exposure not only to GPU developers but also to automation, robotics, autonomous vehicles, and more.

We can see that the top 7 holdings account for more than 50% of the total composition. As for industry types, the ETF is diversified between capital goods, technology, software, health care and semiconductors. The BOTZ ETF has $2.58 billion in net assets and a 0.68% total expense ratio.

Revisiting The Thesis

On January 9, 2025, I published a thesis on the BOTZ ETF with a buy recommendation. The main goal was to recommend a major trend at a fair price, and that seemed clear to me with the BOTZ ETF.

The evolution of AI presupposes advances in AI infrastructure such as GPUs, cloud and data centers, however, what attracted me were the future applications of this: AI agents, smart robotics, automated driving, and biotechnology, but the applications don't stop there.

The BOTZ ETF provides exposure to a wide range of applications. A key takeaway is that more and more areas are being impacted by these innovations, and Morgan Stanley recently corroborated this.

"Humanoid 100" Stock List Updated

Morgan Stanley has updated its "Humanoid 100" stock list, and that says a lot about the broad spectrum of applications for artificial intelligence. The latest update included video game developers and cybersecurity companies.

This includes Take-Two (NASDAQ:TTWO), which will soon release GTA VI. The company will have access to a wealth of data on gamers' activities, and this data can be used to train AI robotics models.

The same applies to cybersecurity applications. The growing adoption of AI tools such as ChatGPT and many others will require a lot of data protection tools. Let's look at the growth data below.

There have been major milestones in the adoption of transformative technologies. As a reference, I bring the below examples such as the Ford Model T in 1908, the iPhone in 2007, and ChatGPT in 2022. We are witnessing something historic, it has never been so quick and easy to have access to a tool that promotes such a gain in productivity. What will it be like with humanoid robots?

To answer the question above, we can refer to another paper from Morgan Stanley. According to the bank's analysts, there could be more than 1 billion humanoid robots by 2050. The projections do not seem unrealistic to me when I analyze growth data.

Fast Adoption Is Impressive

The number of AI models being widely adopted is also growing exponentially, and this has major impacts on the BOTZ ETF thesis and the adoption of AI applications in robotics.

We can break down the graph above and take a closer look at Nvidia's case, which is also the largest holding in the BOTZ ETF. We see that between 2021 and 2025 the number of applications with GPUs multiplied by 2.4x. How much will the growth be between 2025 and 2029?

But when you think that speed of adoption is only happening in technology companies, you are wrong. Below, we have an interesting data point about the mentions of artificial intelligence by S&P 500 companies in their earnings calls.

Interestingly, the technology sector represents 31.6% of the S&P 500, all other companies are in sectors that can have more sophisticated applications with the use of artificial intelligence. But the BOTZ ETF valuation doesn't seem to consider this.

Valuation

The BOTZ ETF is composed of high-growth companies, so we will analyze the valuation by price earnings growth. In the last 3 years, the ETF traded at an average of 4.7x PEG.

We currently have a 3.7x PEG FWD and positive momentum. If it returns to the average of the last 3 years, we have an upside of 27% that supports the recommendation to buy the ETF.

Interestingly, the BOTZ ETF is one of the few AI-related assets that trades with an interesting risk-return ratio. This is because the ETF is less weighted towards technology companies but rather towards their applications in industry.

But what would be the trigger for the BOTZ ETF to also be awarded for its exceptionality? Well, just as AI models like ChatGPT have had exponential adoption, why can't AI applications in robotics, which are just beginning, have that? That's the point.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

Although Morgan Stanley highlights the positive outlook for humanoid robots, analysts predict that demand will only accelerate from 2030 onwards due to increased regulation and social acceptance. Investors are generally impatient, and this could impact prices if it has no impact in the short term.

Additionally, the cost of acquiring humanoid robots is relatively high. The bank projects that the cost will be around $200,000 in 2024, falling in price by 2050. We may see slower adoption than expected due to the high prices.

There is a significant concentration risk, as the 7 main holdings account for more than 50% of the ETF's composition. Last but not least, Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend selling the BOTZ ETF. This is different from my buy recommendation. Investors should carefully consider the risks.

The Bottom Line

While many investors continue to study the applications of artificial intelligence in models and infrastructure, other investors are already looking beyond this and studying the impact that these applications can have on investments.

Applications in robotics and humanoid robots are part of a major phenomenon of exponential growth in artificial intelligence and its branches. The most interesting thing is that investments related to AI applications still have a nice upside, according to the analysis in this article.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy the BOTZ ETF. In my opinion, it is quite clear that the various applications of artificial intelligence, such as robotics and humanoid robots, can present exponential growth soon and reward investors who believed in the thesis.

data.title: Woori Financial: Poised To Ride Korea's Post-Election Tailwinds
data.description:
After turning fully privatized, Woori Financial Group (NYSE:WF), a Korean financial company with businesses spanning Woori Bank, Woori Card, and Woori Investment Bank, among others, has come a long way.

As stellar as Woori’s rate of compounding has been over the last five years, its recent run-up, in line with my previous bullish calls (see Woori Financial: This Rally Has Legs and Woori Financial: This High Yielder Is No Longer A Value Trap), has been particularly strong.

A key reason for this big rally is Korea finally gaining political stability after Jaemyung Lee's (or ‘JM Lee’) and the liberal Democratic Party of Korea's (or 'DPK') election victory. Recall that this comes on the heels of a failed martial law attempt by former President Yoon Suk Yeol, who was subsequently impeached earlier this year – a long-drawn event that created a substantial overhang on Korean equities. The key question, though, now that optimism has been re-priced into markets, is how much upside (if any) remains for the likes of Woori.

Taking Stock as a DPK Term Begins

New President JM Lee’s five-year term begins this month, and early news flow suggests a net positive Korean macro outcome. From a fiscal perspective, DPK is not much different from its Western left-wing counterparts, in that its policies are pro-labor and typically favor higher government spending. Moreso, in times of economic uncertainty like today; note that Korea’s GDP growth estimates have already suffered some big cuts (currently +0.8% YoY per the Korean central bank), in anticipation of tariff-driven disruptions.

Now, Woori’s credit growth fortunes, like its banking counterparts, are tied to the Korean macro; hence, a DPK win is a headline positive. But the finer details, starting with the DPK’s upcoming “extra budget”, are just as important.

First, the negatives, which include the new government’s commitments to amend the Banking Act to reduce loan repayment burdens and waive early repayment penalties at the expense of bank margins. Also, negative is the focus on household debt monitoring, which could limit credit expansion near-term.

That said, the positives are more significant. For one, recent news flow suggests that as part of the DPK’s focus on lower-income groups, cash handouts may be included; thus, a much bigger budget may be on the cards. More fiscal stimulus should, in turn, bolster domestic sentiment, consumption (positive for credit growth) and ease balance sheet stress (positive for asset quality) after months of macro and political turmoil in Korea.

For Woori, the scale of its fiscal stimulus benefits, from better credit growth to asset quality relief (note both its non-performing loan ratio and card delinquency ratios ticked up in Q1 2025) should more than outweigh any hit to margins from banking reforms. A fiscal pickup also limits the central bank’s space for additional interest rate cuts, which would otherwise be dilutive for net interest margins. All in all, net positive read-throughs for Woori.

Governance Reform Catalyst is Back in Play

Beyond the fiscal, there’s a broader corporate governance theme at play as well. Recall that under the previous government, Korea had embarked on a corporate ‘Value-Up’ initiative to drive corporate governance improvements (see EWY: Play The 'Japan 2.0' Theme Via Korea) and, in turn, narrow the persistent ‘Korea Discount’.

Unlike Japan, which more successfully deployed its discount narrowing playbook, Korea’s effort was far less impactful thanks to a divided and unstable government; into end-2024, Woori was dragged down along with the broader Korean indices.

The good news, though, is that this time around, there is a unified government in place, which should allow for more effective reform efforts. And like his predecessor, President JM Lee has been vocal about corporate governance reform, particularly with regard to revising the Commercial Act to extend corporate Boards’ fiduciary duty to common shareholders as well (vs just the "company" prior). Better minority shareholder protection should go a long way toward lifting the ‘Korea Discount’, particularly for the likes of Woori, given its less-than-stellar track record on this front.

The other big reform effort in the pipeline is President JM Lee’s proposal to separate the taxation of dividend income from general income. Korean bank stocks like Woori stand to benefit immensely from this – firstly, because investors pay lower taxes on its elevated 6-7% dividend yield and secondly, by incentivizing Woori to lift its payout ratios (vs the 35% threshold).

Still, recent news flow suggests dividend reform isn’t as much of a certainty, so I wouldn’t underwrite a best-case scenario just yet. But the direction of travel is clearly positive and with five years of political stability ahead of us, there’s a good chance Woori floats on a narrowing ‘Korea Discount’ and re-rates a lot higher from here.

Low Base, Lots of Upside

The combination of fiscal support and Korea's ‘Value-Up’ program getting back on track means that Woori, by delivering on its own “Value Enhancement Plan” commitment, has a clear line of sight into closing its market discount. For context, Woori shares, even after this year’s re-rating, trade at a one-year forward book value multiple of ~0.4x (or ~0.5x this year’s book) – well below the other Korean banking groups.

From a fundamental perspective, Woori is also coming off a low base. Recall that in 2024, dividends were up, but its shareholder return ratio fell to 33.3% (comprising ~29% dividends and ~4% treasury share cancellation) – well below its 50% mid to long-term target. But with capitalization levels improving after the group intentionally pulled back on lending last year, there’s now room to raise its capital return.

For now, though, all eyes will be on post-M&A integration, now that its life insurance acquisitions (TongYang and ABL Life Insurance) have closed. If management delivers on its guidance for a limited CET1 ratio impact and higher non-bank ROEs post-acquisition, while also sustaining its shareholder return expansion trajectory, a re-rating back in line with sector P/B multiples (within the 0.6-0.7x P/B range) is well within reach.

Summing Up

With a supportive new government in place in Korea, Woori looks poised to benefit from a wave of tailwinds. While the stock has also rallied, Woori’s book value discount, the deepest among major Korean banks, leaves space for a lot more upside from here. In the meantime, a well-covered (and growing) 6-7% yield covers the downside. Key catalysts to look out for including the DPK government’s supplementary budget and later in the year, its full 2026 budget, along with the pace of financial market reforms.
